title: Did You Know Your Brain Has a Secret Third Eye?
summary: Imagine a tiny worm-like creature with just ONE eye on top of its head, like a mini cyclops! That's our super-old ancestor from 600 million years ago. Scientists from Lund University and the University of Sussex say all animals with backbones—yes, including you—came from this one-eyed wonder. At first, it had two eyes, but they disappeared when it stopped swimming around. Why keep eyes if you're just sitting still? Later, a single "median eye" formed from light-sensitive cells in the middle of its head. When this creature started swimming again millions of years later, that eye helped build brand-new paired eyes. That's why our eyes are so different from insect or squid eyes. Our retinas (the part that senses light) grew from the brain, not the skin! And here's the coolest part: a leftover piece of that ancient eye is still inside your head. It's called the pineal gland, a tiny light-sensitive organ deep in your brain. So next time you see a cyclops in a movie, remember—you're kind of related!
why_it_matters: A tiny part inside your brain is like a time machine, connecting you to a 600-million-year-old ancestor and showing how your eyes came to be.

keywords.explanation: A one-eyed giant from ancient stories, used here to describe a creature with one eye on top of its head.
A very old relative from long ago, from which later living things evolved.
An eye in the middle of the head, like the one the ancient worm-like creature had.
Able to detect light, like special cells that react when light hits them.
The thin layer at the back of your eye that senses light and helps you see.
A tiny organ deep inside the brain that is still slightly sensitive to light.

questions.question: What did scientists say all animals with backbones came from?
options: A tiny worm-like creature with one eye
questions.correct_answer: A tiny worm-like creature with one eye

questions.question: How long ago did this ancient ancestor live?
Why did the creature's two original eyes disappear?
options: The creature stopped swimming and sat still
They turned into the pineal gland
questions.correct_answer: The creature stopped swimming and sat still

questions.question: Where did our retinas grow from?
What is the leftover piece of the ancient eye called?
What is special about the pineal gland?
options: It helps us see in the dark

background_read: Lund University (a university in Sweden) and the University of Sussex (a university in the UK) worked together on this research.
The pineal gland is sometimes called the 'third eye' because in some animals it can still sense light directly.
Scientists have long debated how complex eyes evolved, and this discovery shows that vertebrate eyes started from brain tissue, not skin.
Article_Structure: WHO: Scientists from Lund University and the University of Sussex
WHAT: They discovered that our ancient worm-like ancestor had a single median eye, and our retinas grew from the brain, leaving the pineal gland as a leftover.
WHERE: In the heads of all modern animals with backbones, including you.
WHY: When the creature started swimming again millions of years later, that old median eye helped build new paired eyes.

perspectives.description: Biologists are excited because this solves a long puzzle about why our eyes are built differently from insect or squid eyes.
Teachers and kids can use this story to explore evolution and realize that parts of our own bodies hold ancient secrets.
Some might think this is just a theory and that more fossil evidence is needed to be completely sure how eyes evolved.
